title: A rigorous result on many-body localization
speaker: John Imbrie
affiliation: University of Virginia; Member, School of Mathematics
abstract: I will discuss a proof of many-body localization for a one-dimensional spin chain with random local interactions. The proof depends on a physically reasonable assumption that limits the amount of level attraction in the system. This is joint work with Tom Spencer.

proof_stages.label: Problem scope and conditional goal
proof_stages.summary: The talk positions rigorous many-body localization as the interacting analogue of Anderson localization and announces a conditional result requiring a limited-level-attraction assumption.

proof_stages.label: Random spin-chain Hamiltonian and reference basis
proof_stages.summary: A one-dimensional random spin-chain Hamiltonian is fixed, with diagonal random-field/Ising terms and small transverse spin-flip terms, so that gamma-zero spin configurations give the unperturbed energies and labels.

proof_stages.label: Eigenstate labeling by spin/metaspin configurations
proof_stages.summary: The desired localization structure is a near-bijection between true eigenstates and unperturbed spin configurations, modified inside sparse resonant intervals into metaspin labels and corresponding local conserved quantities.

proof_stages.label: Limited level attraction hypothesis and theorem
proof_stages.summary: A finite-volume probability bound on very small eigenvalue spacings is assumed, and the main theorem states that this hypothesis implies many-body localization for sufficiently small spin-flip strength gamma.

proof_stages.label: Quantitative content of many-body localization
proof_stages.summary: The conclusion is specified through rare resonant-block estimates, complete diagonalization by local rotations, local observable expectations close to their label values, volume convergence, and decaying truncated correlations/entanglement implying lack of transport.

proof_stages.label: Perturbative rotations in nonresonant regions
proof_stages.summary: In nonresonant regions the proof uses an iterative KAM/RG-style conjugation by antisymmetric generators A=J/(E_i-E_j), cancelling first-order off-diagonal terms and producing rapidly smaller graphical-expansion terms.

proof_stages.label: Block rotations and locality of diagrams
proof_stages.summary: Where energy denominators are small, resonant blocks are diagonalized exactly, while commutator locality restricts the expansion to connected nearest-neighbor diagrams so the rotations remain local.

examples.name: One-body Anderson localization on a lattice
examples.note: The one-particle random Schrödinger model is used as the template from which the site-labeling picture, suppressed random walks, and graphical-expansion method are transferred to spin-configuration space.
examples.engagement_reason: writes the one-body lattice matrix with diagonal potentials and off-diagonal hopping, then reasons through induced random walks, suppression by hopping powers, localization, and eigenstate labeling by sites.

examples.name: Poisson and GOE level statistics
examples.note: Poisson statistics with exponent one and GOE-type level repulsion are given to make the limited-level-attraction hypothesis look natural rather than ad hoc.

examples.name: Two-level avoided crossing and neighboring-level pushing
examples.note: A two-level interaction illustrates ordinary level repulsion, while nearby strongly repelling levels show why small-gap attraction cannot be ruled out by a simple universal principle.
examples.engagement_reason: qualitatively traces the avoided-crossing mechanism: turning on interaction repels two levels, and stronger repulsion from neighboring levels can push weakly interacting levels together.

passes.reason: a minimal few-level avoided-crossing picture is used to show neighboring repulsions can push two levels together
the minimal two-level spectral toy model is used to show that neighboring repulsion can push another pair of levels together

examples.name: Jacobi diagonalization method
examples.note: The classical Jacobi method is used as an analogy for diagonalizing troublesome off-diagonal blocks exactly while perturbatively eliminating the rest.
examples.engagement_reason: develops the Jacobi-style diagonalization concretely by choosing the generator A=J/(Ei−Ej), conjugating the Hamiltonian, computing the commutator cancellation, and iterating to reduce off-diagonal terms.
